title: The Landing Page is Live
excerpt: Playground's new landing page is done. Ideated, designed, and coded it myself in about three weeks while shipping other work in parallel.

sections.activities:
Playground's new landing page website is complete. I ideated the concept, designed every screen, and coded the entire thing myself. HTML, CSS, JavaScript, responsive layouts, animations, all of it. About three weeks from the first sketch to a fully working site, all while keeping my other projects moving.

The process started with rethinking the narrative and information architecture. What story does the landing page need to tell? What should someone feel within the first few seconds of scrolling? From there I moved into high-fidelity design, got feedback from the CEO and the team, iterated, and jumped straight into code. Designing and building it end-to-end meant there was no handoff gap. Every design decision made it into the implementation exactly as I intended.

In parallel, I wrapped up refinements on the background remover and image converter based on post-launch data. We spotted some drop-off in the conversion flow, so I redesigned the confirmation step to be clearer. Small change, but the numbers improved within days.

I also presented a creator dashboard concept I've been developing to the full leadership team. CEO, COO, and engineering leads. 20-minute presentation followed by Q&A. Went really well and the team is excited to move forward with it.
sections.deadlines:
The landing page being done is the biggest milestone of the internship. Going from idea to coded site while managing other active workstreams took real time management but I got it done.

Completed:

  • ✓ Playground landing page, ideated, designed, and coded
  • ✓ Creator dashboard concept presented to leadership
  • ✓ BG remover conversion flow optimization

Next up: Engineering handoff for the creator dashboard, final landing page polish and performance tuning, and documenting my design decisions so the team can keep iterating after my internship ends.
sections.learning:
The landing page project confirmed something I already believed: being able to both design and code is a huge advantage. When you can go from idea to implementation without depending on someone else to translate your vision, the result is better and the process is way faster. Questions like "can we do this animation?" or "is this layout feasible?" I could just try and find out immediately.

I also got better at managing energy across parallel projects. Some days I'd spend the morning on post-launch fixes for the creator tools, the afternoon coding the landing page, and the evening refining the creator dashboard. You have to be intentional about context-switching and know when to go deep versus when to keep things moving.

I've also gotten sharper at knowing when to push for my ideas and when to adapt. Sometimes a good solution shipped today beats a perfect one shipped next month.
sections.environment:
I feel fully part of the team at this point. People come to me with design questions, not just about my projects but about product direction in general. That shift happened naturally over the past few months and it's been cool to see.

Showing the finished landing page to the team was a highlight. A few people were surprised that I designed and built it. There's still this assumption that designers don't code, so breaking that was satisfying.

I've also been helping a newer team member with some design system questions, which is a nice full-circle moment from where I started.
sections.strengths:
Full-stack creative ownership. Took a project from ideation through design to coded implementation with no handoff needed.

Parallel execution. Shipped the landing page while maintaining quality on the creator tools and dashboard work.

Clear communication of design rationale to non-design stakeholders, connecting it to business outcomes.
sections.growth:
Time management when juggling multiple projects at different stages. I sometimes spend too long polishing one thing while another needs attention.

Saying no to new requests when my plate is already full. I take on a lot and need to keep being realistic about capacity.
sections.growthPlan:
I've started time-boxing my work more strictly. Specific hours for each project rather than letting one consume the whole day.

I'm also being more upfront about timelines when new requests come in, rather than just saying yes and figuring it out later.
sections.positive:
If I had to pick one moment that defines this internship, it's refreshing the browser and seeing the finished Playground landing page. Something that three weeks earlier was just a rough idea in my head. I ideated it, designed it, and coded every line of it. That full loop from concept to creation is exactly the kind of work I want to keep doing.

Looking back at where I started, the growth has been real. Twelve weeks ago I was still getting oriented. Now I'm leading presentations, proposing product strategy, and building entire websites from scratch. The team gave me space to run with things and I made the most of it.

title: Shipping and Building
excerpt: The background remover went live, I'm deep into coding Playground's new landing page, and juggling a live product with a major build at the same time.

sections.activities:
The background remover officially launched. Seeing something I designed go live to tens of thousands of creators is a great feeling. Real people are using my work every day now and that's exactly what I'm here for.

Post-launch, I've been monitoring usage data and user feedback. We set up analytics on the key flows and I've been tracking completion rates, error states, and drop-off points. Already surfaced a few areas where the UX could be smoother.

The image converter is in final QA and should launch next week. I've been working with the engineering team to resolve some edge cases around file format support and making sure the error messages actually help people instead of confusing them.

Most of my energy has been going into the Playground landing page redesign. Designs are locked in after feedback from leadership, and I've moved into the coding phase. I'm building it out myself. HTML, CSS, JavaScript, responsive behavior, animations, the whole thing. Doing this alongside the creator tools work is a lot, but going from a blank Figma artboard to a working interactive website is exactly my kind of challenge.
sections.deadlines:
Biggest milestone: background remover shipped on schedule. Felt good after weeks of iteration.

Status update:

  • ✓ Background remover, live
  • ✓ Post-launch monitoring and quick fixes
  • → Image converter, in QA, launching next week
  • → Playground landing page, designs finalized, actively coding

Coming up: Image converter launch support, finishing the landing page build, and polishing responsive behavior and performance. Aiming to have the landing page coded out within the next couple of weeks.
sections.learning:
Big takeaway from these two weeks: designing a feature is only half the job. Supporting it after launch, watching how people actually use it, responding to feedback, fixing issues, that's a completely different skill set and I'm getting better at it.

On the landing page, coding my own designs has been really useful for my growth as a designer. Some things I designed in Figma looked great but were tricky to implement well. Complex responsive layouts, smooth scroll animations, performance-conscious image loading. Solving those problems myself means I now think about buildability while I'm still in the design phase.

Had a good conversation with my mentor about design maturity. Early on you focus on making things look good, then you learn to make things work well, then you learn to make the right things. Bridging design and code is speeding that up for me.
sections.environment:
Post-launch energy in the office has been great. Seeing real usage numbers go up brings the team together. We celebrated when the background remover hit its first-day usage target.

The landing page work has been more independent compared to the collaborative feature work. Heads-down coding for long stretches, then sharing progress with the team for feedback. A few engineers have been impressed that I'm doing the implementation myself, which is cool.

Atlanta is starting to feel like home. Found my regular coffee shop, figured out the best lunch spots near the office, and joined a weekend basketball pickup game with some of the engineers.
sections.strengths:
Working across design and code. Going from concept to implementation without a handoff gap.

Managing parallel workstreams. Shipping creator tools while building the landing page at the same time.

Strong relationships across teams, design, engineering, and data.
sections.growth:
Documenting design decisions more proactively. I tend to keep rationale in my head rather than writing it down, which makes it harder for others to build on my work later.

Knowing when to stop polishing and ship. I can get caught up perfecting animations and micro-interactions when the bigger picture is already solid.
sections.growthPlan:
Started keeping a simple decision log for each project. What I chose, why, and what alternatives I considered. Takes five minutes but saves hours of re-explaining later.

For the landing page, I set a hard deadline and a checklist of must-haves vs. nice-to-haves. If it's not on the must-have list, it waits until after launch.
sections.positive:
Watching real creators use the background remover and seeing positive feedback on social media has been wild. One creator posted a video saying it saved them "hours of work." That's the kind of impact I'm here for.

And sitting in the office late with headphones on, coding the landing page and watching it come to life section by section, that's been one of my favorite parts of this entire internship. There's a flow state you get into when you're building something you designed yourself. Can't wait to share the finished product.

title: From Design to Development
excerpt: Shipped the Alice landing page, handed off creator tool designs to engineering, and kicked off the biggest project yet: redesigning Playground's main landing page from scratch.

sections.activities:
These two weeks have been about the transition from design to development. The background remover and image converter designs are finalized, and I've been working closely with engineering to make sure everything translates properly from Figma to code.

This meant creating detailed design specifications. Spacing, interactions, edge cases, error states, empty states, responsive behavior. Not the most exciting work but it makes a huge difference in whether the final product matches the design.

The Alice landing page launched earlier this period. My first shipped project at Playground. The team was happy with the results and we're already seeing solid engagement metrics.

The biggest thing though is a new project that's become my main focus: redesigning Playground's main landing page website. The current site needed a refresh and I pitched a new direction to leadership. They were into it, so now I'm leading the whole thing, concept to final implementation. The plan is to not just design it but code it out myself. These first two weeks I've been deep in ideation. Studying competitor sites, defining the narrative structure, exploring visual directions, and mapping out the information architecture.

I also ran usability tests on the background remover flow with a small group of creators. Got solid feedback that led to a few meaningful iterations before the feature ships.
sections.deadlines:
Alice landing page is shipped. Good to have that one out the door.

Status update:

  • ✓ Alice landing page, shipped and live
  • ✓ Background remover, design specs complete, in development
  • ✓ Image converter, design specs complete, development starting
  • → Playground landing page, ideation and early concepts underway
  • ✓ Usability testing round 1, completed with iterations

Coming up: Moving the landing page redesign into high-fidelity design, supporting engineering through creator tools implementation, and QA reviews on the background remover build.
sections.learning:
Biggest learning was around design handoff. Clean Figma files aren't enough. There's so much context that doesn't live in the mockups. Why certain decisions were made, how things should behave at different screen sizes, what happens in error states. Writing good specs is a real skill and I'm getting better at it.

Starting the landing page project also got me thinking about design at the brand level. A product landing page isn't just UI, it's storytelling. How do you communicate what Playground is and why someone should care, all within a few seconds of scrolling? Very different from feature design work.

Working with engineers on the creator tools, I've gotten a better grasp of technical constraints. Why certain animations might cause performance issues, why a simple-looking design might be complex to build. That knowledge is already shaping how I'm approaching the landing page. I want to design something I can build well.
sections.environment:
My relationship with engineering has grown a lot. Sitting near them in the office means I can answer questions quickly when they're implementing my designs, and they teach me how things work under the hood.

There's a good back-and-forth between design and engineering. Sometimes I push for a detail that matters for the experience, sometimes I accept a simpler implementation to get to launch faster. That balance is something I'm getting more comfortable with.

Getting the green light on the landing page project from leadership felt like a strong vote of confidence. The team values what I bring and they're giving me room to run with it.
sections.strengths:
Thorough design specifications that reduce back-and-forth during development.

Taking initiative on the landing page project. Identified the opportunity and pitched it rather than waiting for it to be assigned.

Adapting quickly when user testing surfaces issues. Not getting attached to initial solutions.
sections.growth:
Scoping a large project realistically. The landing page is ambitious and I need to deliver it alongside my other work.

Being more decisive when there are multiple valid approaches. I sometimes spend too long evaluating options when either would work fine.
sections.growthPlan:
For the landing page, I created a rough timeline broken into phases: ideation (done), design, code, and polish. Those checkpoints help me stay honest about progress.

For decisiveness, I'm giving myself time limits for low-stakes decisions. If two options are roughly equal, pick one and move forward.
sections.positive:
The Alice landing page going live was a big moment. Took a screenshot on my phone and sent it to my family. Designing something in Figma is one thing. Seeing it on a real website that real people visit hits different.

And then getting the go-ahead on the Playground landing page redesign on top of that. The team trusts me with increasingly important work and I'm making the most of that trust. Going from designing a feature component to owning the company's front door to the world is a big step.

title: Deep in the Details
excerpt: Finalizing the Alice landing page, going high-fidelity on the creator tools, and running my first usability sessions with real users.

sections.activities:
These two weeks were some of the most heads-down design work so far. The Alice landing page is nearly done. We went through several rounds of feedback with leadership and iterated on the messaging, visual hierarchy, and call-to-action placement. Good exercise in refining a design through multiple review cycles.

On the creator tools side, I've been working on high-fidelity designs for the background remover. Moving beyond wireframes into fully realized UI. Colors, typography, spacing, interaction states, all within Playground's design system. Getting the details right at this stage matters because these are the files engineers will actually build from.

I also started designing the image converter flow in more detail. The challenge is keeping it simple when there are many possible input and output formats. I've been exploring different approaches to surface the most common conversions while still handling edge cases.

A highlight was running my first informal usability session. Showed the background remover prototypes to a few creators who use Playground regularly and got their honest reactions. The feedback led to some solid changes I wouldn't have caught on my own.
sections.deadlines:
The Alice landing page is in final review. One more round of tweaks and it's ready for development handoff next week.

Progress:

  • ✓ Alice landing page, final design iteration
  • ✓ Background remover, high-fidelity designs in progress
  • → Image converter, detailed flow mapping underway
  • ✓ First informal usability session completed

Next up: Finishing the background remover high-fidelity mockups, Alice handoff to engineering, and planning a more structured round of user testing for the creator tools.
sections.learning:
Biggest takeaway was about user feedback. I had assumptions about how the background remover should work, and watching actual creators use the prototype challenged some of them. I thought users would want granular control over the removal process, but most just wanted a one-click solution. Good reminder to test early and often.

I also got better at designing within constraints. Playground's design system is comprehensive, which is great for consistency but sometimes means I can't do exactly what I want visually. Working creatively within those boundaries is a useful skill to build.

On the practical side, I've been improving how I manage multiple design files and keep my Figma workspace organized. When you're working on three features at the same time, good file hygiene saves a lot of time.
sections.environment:
The team rhythm is really clicking now. I know when people are available for quick feedback, when to schedule deeper reviews, and when to just put my head down and work.

I've been having more direct conversations with engineers about the features I'm designing. Understanding their perspective early, what's easy, what's hard, what's risky, helps me make better design decisions before handoff instead of running into problems later.

Atlanta in spring is beautiful. I've been exploring different neighborhoods on weekends and found a great running trail near Piedmont Park.
sections.strengths:
Attention to detail in high-fidelity design. The team has commented on the polish level of my mockups.

Seeking out and acting on user feedback, even when it challenges my initial assumptions.

More comfortable presenting work-in-progress to stakeholders without needing everything to be perfect first.
sections.growth:
Structuring usability sessions more formally. My first one was useful but a bit loose. I want to get better at writing discussion guides and capturing findings in a consistent way.

Managing scope. I sometimes go deeper on details than necessary at a given stage, spending time on pixel-perfect polish when the design direction itself still needs validation.
sections.growthPlan:
For usability testing, I've been reading about different research methods and plan to create a simple template for future sessions. Goals, tasks, questions, and a consistent way to document findings.

For scope management, I'm being more explicit about what stage a design is in (exploration, refinement, or final) and matching my level of detail to that stage.
sections.positive:
Hearing a creator say "oh, this is exactly what I needed" during the usability session was probably the highlight of my internship so far. Easy to lose sight of real users when you're deep in pixels, so those moments are motivating.

I've hit a good stride with the team. I'm not the new person figuring things out anymore. I'm someone who contributes to product discussions and whose design opinions carry weight. That feels good and I plan to keep building on it.

title: From Ideas to Implementation
excerpt: Working on new feature concepts, designing the landing page for Alice, and transitioning from remote work to joining the team in Atlanta.

sections.activities:
Over the past two weeks the work has started becoming more concrete.

I've continued exploring potential creator tools, particularly features like background removal and image conversion. The focus has been translating these ideas into actual product flows and interface concepts. This involved wireframing different approaches and thinking through how these tools would fit into the existing Playground interface.

Another major task has been designing the landing page for our upcoming AI assistant called Alice. The goal is to communicate what Alice does in a clear and simple way while staying consistent with the Playground visual identity.

At the same time, I've been monitoring template usage and product metrics, looking at which templates are being used most and trying to understand patterns in how creators interact with the platform.

I've also been helping a bit with the operations side of the creator program, which gave me a better understanding of the relationship between the platform and its creator community.

A big personal milestone during this period was also arriving in Atlanta and starting to work with the team in person, which makes collaboration much easier.
sections.deadlines:
Some of the feature concepts are still being validated, but the direction is becoming clearer.

The Alice landing page is moving toward completion, and the next step will be refining the design and preparing it for development.

For the next few weeks, the focus will likely be on finalizing feature concepts and supporting developers during implementation.
sections.learning:
One interesting realization from these weeks was how important product data is when making design decisions.

Looking at template metrics gave useful context around what creators actually use on the platform. Sometimes the most valuable insights come from observing real behavior rather than purely designing new ideas.

Another big shift was working in person with the team after the first remote weeks. Being able to quickly discuss ideas and iterate together speeds up the design process significantly.
sections.environment:
The team environment is very open and discussion-driven.

Because Playground operates at a relatively large scale, many conversations revolve around how decisions will affect existing users, which adds an interesting layer to product design.

Being able to join leadership discussions and product conversations has been very valuable for understanding how different perspectives influence product decisions.
sections.strengths:
I feel comfortable jumping into product discussions and quickly translating ideas into visual concepts and flows that the team can react to.

My previous experience working with startups has also helped when it comes to adapting to fast-moving conversations and shifting priorities.
sections.growth:
One area I want to improve is structuring presentations of ideas more clearly, especially when several possible approaches exist.

Another is continuing to build a deeper understanding of the data and metrics side of product decisions, since those insights often guide which features should be prioritized.
sections.growthPlan:
To improve this, I've been spending more time reviewing product metrics and asking questions about how the team interprets the data.

I'm also trying to frame design ideas more clearly during discussions by explaining the problem, the reasoning behind the solution, and the expected impact.
sections.positive: These weeks felt like a transition from onboarding into actively contributing to product decisions. It's exciting to start seeing how early design ideas can evolve into features that might eventually be used by thousands of creators.

title: Starting at Playground
excerpt: First weeks getting familiar with the product, the team, and how new features move from idea to implementation at scale.

sections.activities:
The first couple of weeks were mainly about getting a deeper understanding of Playground as a product and how the team approaches building new features.

Since the internship focuses on designing and validating new product features, I started by getting involved in discussions around potential creator tools, including features like background removal and image conversion. My role has been helping explore how these tools could fit into the existing product experience. That involved conversations with the CEO and COO, thinking through possible implementations, and starting early wireframes.

Because Playground is used by a large number of creators every month, a big part of the work is making sure new features integrate naturally into the existing workflows rather than just adding more tools.

Alongside that, I started looking into template usage and creator activity, monitoring which templates are performing well and how creators interact with them. This helped give context for how the product is actually being used.

Most of my work so far has been around product thinking and early design exploration — mapping out flows, wireframing potential solutions, and discussing how ideas could realistically be implemented.
sections.deadlines:
The first weeks were more about exploration and alignment than strict deadlines.

Because new features need to work within an existing platform used by many people, the early stage is focused on making sure ideas make sense before committing development resources.

The goal over the next few weeks is to refine the concepts we've been exploring and move them toward clear prototypes and implementation plans.
sections.learning:
One thing that stood out quickly is how different it is designing inside an established product compared to building something from scratch.

At Playground, every feature needs to work within an existing design system, product logic, and technical constraints. Decisions also need to consider the impact on a large user base.

Having previous experience working with startups definitely helps here, because the discussions are often very product-oriented. The focus is less on isolated design decisions and more on how a feature affects the broader ecosystem of the platform.

It's also been interesting to see how quickly ideas move from casual discussions into something that might actually ship.
sections.environment:
The first part of the internship was remote, but I was still able to join product discussions and meetings with the team.

The environment is very collaborative and fairly direct. Ideas are discussed openly, and it's common to iterate quickly based on feedback.

I've also been able to join some leadership discussions, which gives a broader perspective on how the company thinks about product direction and growth.
sections.strengths:
One thing that has helped a lot so far is being comfortable working in early product discussions. Because I've previously worked with startup teams, I'm used to situations where ideas are still forming and need to be translated into something concrete.

I've also been able to move fairly quickly from discussions into wireframes and flows, which helps make abstract ideas easier for the team to evaluate.
sections.growth:
One area I want to improve is communicating design decisions more clearly in group discussions. Even when the ideas are solid, presenting them in a way that is concise and easy for everyone to evaluate is an important skill.

Another area is documenting flows and product decisions more clearly, especially since features eventually need to be implemented by developers.
sections.growthPlan:
To improve this, I've been making an effort to structure my thinking before presenting ideas, focusing more on the reasoning behind decisions rather than only the design outcome.

I've also started documenting flows and feature ideas more clearly so the team can reference them later during implementation.
sections.positive: These first weeks have been a strong introduction to the product and how the team works. I'm looking forward to moving from early exploration into more concrete prototypes and feature validation in the coming weeks.
